page.content: The following data table presents a comprehensive analysis of the optical coherence tomography (OCT) metrics comparing preoperative measurements with those taken 90 days post-surgery. This comparison is crucial for assessing the efficacy of the surgical intervention on visual function and retinal health. Key metrics evaluated include the correlation coefficient (ICC) and the bias percentage, which provide insights into the reliability and accuracy of the measurements taken at both time points.

preoperative measurements with those taken 90 days post-surgery. This comparison is crucial for assessing the efficacy of the
surgical intervention on visual function and retinal health. Key metrics evaluated include the correlation coefficient (ICC) and
the bias percentage, which provide insights into the reliability and accuracy of the measurements taken at both time points.

page.content: Notably, the ICC value of 0.853 indicates a strong level of agreement between the two sets of data, while the bias percentage reflects the systematic differences observed, with values ranging from −5.48% to 6.75%. Additionally, the p value from the Wilcoxon test, recorded at 0.010, suggests that the differences observed are statistically significant, reinforcing the importance of these findings in clinical practice. The metrics of mean square (MS) and mean difference (MD) further elucidate the variations in visual acuity, specifically in relation to best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) outcomes. Overall, this analysis underscores the critical role of these OCT measurements in evaluating postoperative recovery.
